symbols: raised arms of Jeremiah — prophetic invocation, the prophet as mediator between God and the people, channeling divine revelation
burst of light from above — the word of the Lord coming to Jeremiah, divine inspiration and the fire of prophetic utterance
gathered crowd in various postures — the range of human responses to prophetic preaching: attentiveness, skepticism, anguish, contemplation
Jeremiah's white garment — prophetic purity and the priestly lineage of Jeremiah, son of Hilkiah the priest
dark architectural setting — the spiritual darkness of a nation that has turned from God, into which the prophet speaks light
description: A dramatic monochrome drawing in brown wash and ink, depicting the prophet Jeremiah standing at center with both arms raised high in prophetic proclamation. He wears flowing white robes and his face is turned upward toward a brilliant burst of light that breaks through clouds above. Around him, a crowd of followers and listeners sits, kneels, or stands in various attitudes — some gazing at the prophet, some with heads bowed, some appearing troubled or amazed. The composition radiates outward from Jeremiah's upraised figure. An architectural wall or doorway frames the scene on the left.
theological_description: Gustave Dore captures the essence of Jeremiah's prophetic ministry — the 'weeping prophet' who was called before birth to be 'a prophet to the nations' (Jeremiah 1:5). God placed his words in Jeremiah's mouth as 'a fire' and the people as 'wood' to be consumed (Jeremiah 5:14). The raised arms recall God's original commission: 'Do not be afraid of them, for I am with you to deliver you' (Jeremiah 1:8). Jeremiah's ministry was marked by deep tension: he loved his people yet was compelled to pronounce judgment upon them for their idolatry and covenant-breaking. The burst of heavenly light above the prophet visualizes the recurring formula 'The word of the Lord came to me' (Jeremiah 1:4, 2:1, and dozens of occurrences throughout the book). The varied postures of the crowd reflect the mixed reception Jeremiah received — some believed, but the majority rejected his message, and the prophet suffered imprisonment, plots against his life, and the agony of watching Jerusalem fall. Yet Jeremiah also proclaimed the great hope of the New Covenant: 'I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts' (Jeremiah 31:33), a passage that becomes foundational for New Testament theology (Hebrews 8:8-12). The prophet stands as a type of Christ himself — rejected by his own people, weeping over Jerusalem, yet faithfully delivering God's word to the end.

scripture_refs.reason: The call of Jeremiah as a prophet to the nations, the foundational commission behind his preaching ministry.
God making his words in Jeremiah's mouth a fire — the prophetic utterance visualized in the heavenly light.
Jeremiah commanded to stand in the temple court and speak to all the people, the setting suggested by the architectural frame.
The New Covenant promise — I will write my law on their hearts — the great hope within Jeremiah's ministry of judgment.
The New Testament appropriation of Jeremiah's New Covenant prophecy as fulfilled in Christ.
His word is in my heart like a fire shut up in my bones — the compulsion to prophesy despite persecution.
